text: His name is Morpheus.
He has two pills. One in each hand.
"You take the blue pill — the story ends. You wake up in your bed and believe whatever you want to believe."
"You take the red pill — you stay in Wonderland. And I show you how deep the rabbit hole goes."
He watches you the way people watch someone who is about to make a mistake they cannot undo.
You wake up.
Your bed. Your apartment.
It worked.
You can feel it — the absence of something you can't name.
Like a tooth that stopped aching.
You don't know why your hand is shaking.
You decide not to think about it.
White. Infinite white.
"This is the construct. A loading program."
Morpheus explains everything. The machines. The harvest. The dream you were living.
Your muscles have atrophied. Your eyes have never focused in real light before.
But here, in the white, anything can exist.
He asks you: how do you want to learn?

choices.label: Push hard. You want every program, as fast as they can load.
Take it slow. You want to understand before you act.

text: You wake up.
Your alarm hasn't gone off yet. 6:47 AM. Thirteen minutes early.
The light is the same light it always is.
Your apartment is exactly as you left it.
You lie there waiting for something to feel wrong.
Nothing does.
That's the thing about relief. It feels exactly like being right.
Metacortex. Floor 7. Cubicle 9.
Your coffee is bad. Your code is good.
The cursor blinks at the correct time. The clock never says 2:13 AM.
Your boss, Mr. Rhineheart, stops at your desk on Tuesday.
He says: "Anderson. I don't know what changed. But keep it up."
He doesn't wait for an answer. He never does.
You write four hundred clean lines before lunch.
You do not think about Morpheus. You do not think about the red pill.
You think about whether to get the sandwich or the soup.
You get the soup.
It is good.
Six weeks later. A coffee shop two blocks from work.
You are reading something. You don't remember what.
You feel it before you see it — the specific weight of being watched.
She is across the room.
Trinity.
She is not pretending not to see you. She is just... looking.
Still. Patient. Like someone saying goodbye to a city they're leaving.
Then she stands up.
She is going to leave.
She has not spoken a single word.
You catch her half a block down. She doesn't look surprised.
"I'm not here to change your mind," she says. She doesn't stop walking.
"Then why are you here?"
She slows. Just slightly.
"I needed to see that you were okay."
She says it the way you say a thing you don't quite believe.
You almost ask: are you okay?
But she turns the corner.
And she is gone.
And you stand on a sidewalk in the middle of a Tuesday and you are very, very okay.
You walk back to your coffee.
It has gone cold.

choices.label: Go home. Something is stuck in your head.

text: It starts small.
The man in the elevator — you've seen him every day for six weeks. Third floor. Never gets off on third floor.
The pigeon on your windowsill. Same pigeon. Same branch of the same scar on its right wing.
The homeless man on Fifth says: "Brother, can you spare a—" and then stops.
He said the same thing yesterday. Different order.
You start writing it down.
Not code. Just observations.
You fill half a notebook.
Then the news comes on.
"...the fugitive known only as Morpheus was apprehended late Thursday..."
The photo is blurry. But it's him.
Sunglasses. Leather coat. The posture of someone who knew this was coming and came anyway.
"...believed to be connected to a series of domestic cyberattacks..."
You are standing in front of your television holding a fork.
You don't know when you stood up.
His name hits you somewhere below the sternum. Like a word in a language you forgot you spoke.
Your hands are shaking.
You put the fork down very carefully.
You sit with the shaking for a long time.

choices.label: Five years. Let five years pass.

text: The promotion comes through on a Thursday.
New title. New office. A window that faces east — you get the sunrise every morning.
Your coworkers take you to a bar. Someone orders a bottle of something expensive.
You toast. You smile. You mean it.
That night, alone in your apartment, the phone rings.
You answer.
Silence. Then breathing. Then a click.
Wrong number.
You sit with the dial tone for longer than makes sense.
You don't know what you were waiting for.
You hang up.
You make tea.
You go to bed.

choices.label: Five years. Let five years pass.

text: FIVE YEARS LATER.
You are Senior Developer. You have an apartment in a better building.
You have routines you love: the 5k every morning, the diner on Saturdays, the same corner table.
You have a woman named Claire who is warm and funny and who you are fond of, genuinely.
You do not think about the Matrix.
You do not think about that word. You don't know why you know that word.
Claire says you talk in your sleep sometimes.
She says you say things she doesn't understand.
She says it like it's charming.
You smile and change the subject.
You are good at changing the subject.

choices.label: The dream comes. It always comes.

text: There is a hallway.
You have never been in this hallway.
At the end: a red phone. Old. Rotary dial. Ringing.
You answer it.
Silence. Then: "You have to see this."
You don't recognize the voice. You recognize the voice.
You wake up at 2:13 AM.
Your laptop is open.
You have been writing code in your sleep.
Hundreds of lines. Clean. Efficient.
You don't know what it does.
You almost close the laptop.
You don't.
It's a network analysis tool.
Elegant. Obsessive. It maps traffic patterns you've never thought to look for.
You let it run against Metacortex's internal logs.
Everything routes through three server addresses.
You've worked here for eight years. You have never seen these addresses documented.
You search internal wikis. Nothing.
You search external databases. Nothing.
You dig deeper.
You should not have dug deeper.
The addresses resolve to... nothing.
Not private. Not classified. Not firewalled.
Not there.
They are addresses for servers that should not exist.
You sit in your kitchen at 3 AM and the sunrise finds you still sitting there.

choices.label: Find Trinity's old contact. Write to her.

text: You find the address from memory.
You don't know how you remember it. You remember it perfectly.
Encrypted relay. Eight hops.
You write three drafts. You delete two.
The third says: "I think I made a mistake."
Six words.
You hover over SEND.
Outside, a car passes.
The city breathes.
Your cursor blinks.

choices.label: Delete it. Close the laptop. Go to bed.

text: The reply comes in forty seconds.
As if she was watching the relay. Waiting.
"We know. We've been watching."
Then: "Neo — Morpheus is gone. He died in custody. Three years ago."
Then: "The One we needed — we needed him five years ago."
Then: "The door is still open. But it is almost closed."
You read it four times.
Then you read it a fifth.
Then you stand up and walk to your window.
The city is the same city it always was.
You know now that it isn't.
You know now that it never was.
You close your eyes.
You type back: "Tell me where to go."
You are older now. Slower. Five years of softness built into you.
But the door is open.
And this time you do not hesitate.
Cold.
Colder than you expected. Colder than the word cold describes.
Metal. Tubes. The sound of something that has held you your entire life finally letting go.
You open your eyes in a body you have used — but never truly owned.
The lights are harsh. Everything is rust and wire and a silence so complete it has texture.
Trinity is there.
She is older too. There are lines around her eyes that weren't there before.
She doesn't say welcome.
She says: "You're here."
Like that's enough.
Like that might actually be enough.
You think about the five years.
You think about the soup.
You think about Claire, who will wake up tomorrow and find you gone.
You think about what it costs to arrive somewhere this late.
Then you sit up.
And you begin.
You delete it.
You close the laptop.
You make coffee.
You go to bed.
You sleep, and you do not dream.
Twenty years later you have a daughter named Sarah and a son named James and a mortgage on a house with a yard.
You run 5k every morning. Your knees hurt now, but you run.
You retire at sixty-two with a party and a cake and people who genuinely love you.
Your grandchildren think you're funny.
You are, by every measurable standard, happy.
You never think about the red phone.
You never think about the word Matrix.
You never think about what the three server addresses meant.
You are the perfect prisoner.
Not because you are kept here.
Because you stopped wanting to leave.
The most dangerous thing the Matrix ever did was make peace feel like freedom.
You want more.
Every program Tank loads — you ask for the next one before it finishes.
Jiu jitsu. Krav maga. Drunken boxing. Tactical weapons. Flight simulation.
Tank watches you from behind the monitor. He doesn't say anything for a while.
Then: "Most people take weeks, you know."
You don't answer. You're already running the next sequence.
You feel it — the belief building like pressure behind your eyes.
You can do anything in here.
That's the problem.
In here, you have never been wrong.
You are starting to forget that out there is different.

choices.label: Go see the Oracle. You're ready.

text: You take it one program at a time.
Not because you're afraid. Because you want to understand.
You stop mid-sequence and ask Tank to replay segments at a quarter speed.
You map the rules before you try to break them.
Morpheus watches from the door one afternoon and doesn't say anything.
Later he tells Trinity: "He's different."
She says: "Different how?"
He thinks about it.
"Like he's reading the water before he steps in."
You don't know he said this.
But you have started to see the seams.
Small things.
The way physics hesitates before it catches up.
The gap between what should happen and what does.
You write it all down.

choices.label: You're ready. Go see the Oracle.

text: "I'm going alone."
Trinity stares at you. "That's not happening."
"They'll be expecting both of us. One person is harder to track."
"One person is easier to kill."
You argue for eleven minutes.
You go anyway.
The lobby is different when you're alone.
The marble sounds different underfoot.
The guards are real in a way they weren't before — they have weight.
You take three hits you shouldn't have taken.
You find Morpheus on the thirty-first floor.
He looks at you with something you'll think about later.
Something that looks almost like relief.
"You came alone," he says.
"Yes."
"That was stupid."
"I know."
He almost smiles.
"Let's go."
You go back.
Her kitchen. The smell of cookies. The same chair.
She looks at you like she knew you'd come.
"I know why you're here," she says.
"I need to understand why I'm going to go in after him."
She pours tea. Sets a cup in front of you.
"You already know why."
"Tell me anyway."
She's quiet for a moment.
Then she says something.
What she says depends on who you've become.

choices.label: "Because if I don't go, who does?"
"I don't know. I just know I have to."

text: Smith moves.
You move first.
You've seen this in the construct — the arc of a strike, the weight shift, the tell.
You dodge.
Too early.
Smith doesn't go where you predicted.
He adjusts mid-motion. Something no human body should do.
His fist finds your jaw.
The roof tiles are hard. The sky above them is very blue.
You lie there for a moment that stretches.
He stands over you.
"Interesting," he says. The word sounds like a file being created.
You think: I was so sure.
You think: being sure is not the same as being right.
You get up anyway.

choices.label: Get up. Fight smarter this time.

text: Smith moves.
You watch him move.
You've seen this — not in training, not in instinct.
You've seen it in your notes. The gap you wrote down. The hesitation between intention and execution.
Forty milliseconds.
Every Agent has it. The physics of the simulation catching up to the decision.
You wait for the gap.
It opens.
You step through it.
Smith's strike passes through air where you were.
Your palm finds his sternum.
The sound he makes is not a human sound.
He staggers.
You don't.
The fight is short after that.
Not because you are stronger.
Because you understood the rules well enough to find the one that didn't quite hold.
